title: John
author: John the apostle, son of Zebedee (the 'beloved disciple')
setting: Written late in the first century, probably at Ephesus, to a mixed Jewish-Greek audience. John supplements the Synoptics rather than repeating them, and writes with explicit theological intent.
purpose: Stated plainly in John 20:31: 'these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.' John presents the most direct christological claims in all the Gospels.
themes: Light and darkness, life and death

before.label: In the beginning was the Word — John 1:1 echoes Genesis 1:1
Isaiah's vision of YHWH's glory — John 12:41 applies it to Jesus
Expulsion from the synagogue — John's community context

after.label: Resurrection appearances; Thomas confesses 'My Lord and my God'
Pentecost — the Paraclete arrives as Jesus promised
1 John — the Johannine community applies the gospel

key_people.name: Jesus Christ
key_people.role: The pre-existent Logos who becomes flesh; John's 'I am' declarations (bread of life, light of the world, good shepherd, resurrection and life, way, truth, life, true vine) provide the book's christological architecture

key_people.name: The Beloved Disciple
key_people.role: The unnamed disciple who reclines at Jesus' breast at the Last Supper, stands at the cross, and first believes at the empty tomb — the ideal witness and model for the Johannine community

key_people.name: Mary Magdalene
key_people.role: First witness of the risen Jesus (20:11-18) and the first person commissioned to announce the resurrection — her apostolic role is explicit and prominent in John

key_people.name: Thomas
key_people.role: The doubting disciple whose confession 'My Lord and my God' (20:28) is the book's christological climax — the highest explicit Christology in all four gospels

context: John was written c. AD 90-100, likely in Ephesus, for a community that had suffered expulsion from the synagogue (9:22; 12:42; 16:2). It is theologically the most developed of the four gospels, written by a community that has been living with the tradition for decades and has meditated deeply on its meaning. The book contains no birth narrative, no temptation, no Transfiguration, and no institution of the Supper — instead, a Prologue that places Jesus within the eternal divine life.
christ_connection: John's Christology is the highest in the NT: the Logos who was with God and was God (1:1), the I AM who predates Abraham (8:58), and the one who shares glory with the Father from before the world existed (17:5). The seven signs (2:1-11; 4:46-54; 5:1-15; 6:1-15; 6:16-21; 9:1-12; 11:1-44) are not merely miracles but epiphanies — moments when the glory of the incarnate Son of God becomes visible. The Farewell Discourse (chs. 14-17) is Jesus' most extensive self-disclosure in the Gospels, and the High Priestly Prayer (ch. 17) gives unparalleled access to the inner life of the Son's relationship with the Father.

key_verses.note: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God' — the book's opening declaration of the eternal pre-existence and divine identity of Jesus Christ
'For God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life' — the most memorised verse in Scripture; the gospel in miniature
'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live' — Jesus' self-identification before the greatest sign, the raising of Lazarus
Thomas's confession 'My Lord and my God!' — the book's christological climax, the highest Christology in any gospel, elicited by the physical evidence of the resurrection

themes_detail.title: The Incarnation and the Glory of God Made Visible
themes_detail.text: John 1:14 is the theological heart of the book: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory.' The Tabernacle/Temple imagery is deliberate — the Shekinah glory that filled the wilderness tabernacle and Solomon's temple has now taken up residence in a human body. The seven signs are not just miracles but theophanic events — moments when the eternal glory of the Son of God becomes visible through specific, concrete acts. Each sign is accompanied by teaching that explains what kind of glory is being revealed.

themes_detail.title: Belief and Unbelief as the Book's Central Division
themes_detail.text: John's gospel is structured by the response to Jesus: some believe and receive life; others reject and remain in darkness. The pattern repeats through the signs and discourses: the disciples believe (2:11); the religious leaders reject (5:18; 7:1; 10:31-39); 'the world' receives its own judgment by its response to the light (3:19-21). This is not fatalism but a call to response: John's stated purpose (20:30-31) is to produce belief, and the entire book is organized as an extended invitation to trust the one who is the resurrection and the life.

themes_detail.title: The Holy Spirit as the Continuing Presence of Jesus
themes_detail.text: The Paraclete sayings in the Farewell Discourse (14:16-17, 26; 15:26-27; 16:7-15) are John's most developed pneumatology. The Spirit is 'another Paraclete' (14:16) — the same kind of helping presence that Jesus has been, continuing his ministry after the ascension. The Spirit will teach, remind, guide into truth, testify about Jesus, and convict the world. John's understanding of the Spirit as the personal continuation of Jesus' presence gives the Christian community a basis for confident life after the departure of the historical Jesus.

themes_detail.title: The Father-Son Relationship as the Model for Discipleship
themes_detail.text: John contains more teaching about the relationship between the Father and the Son than all the other gospels combined. The Son does nothing of himself but only what he sees the Father doing (5:19); he speaks not his own words but the Father's (14:24); he does the works the Father gives him (17:4). This total dependence and perfect obedience is not the Son's subordination but the pattern of all true human life before God — the image of God fully expressed in a human being. The disciples are invited into this same relationship: 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (20:21).
